{"success": true, "name": "visual-ui-audit", "description": "Systematic visual UX audit of a live website using DOM analysis + screenshots — identifies spacing/typography/contrast/image/locale issues with concrete CSS-to-fix output.", "tags": ["ux", "audit", "visual", "screenshots", "css", "frontend", "review"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: visual-ui-audit description: "Systematic visual UX audit of a live website using DOM analysis + screenshots — identifies spacing/typography/contrast/image/locale issues with concrete CSS-to-fix output." version: 2.1.0 tags: [ux, audit, visual, screenshots, css, frontend, review] --- # Visual UI Audit — Systematic Framework ## When to Use After any deploy, content update, or design iteration. Run when: - The user asks "analyze visually and critique" or provides an audit prompt/format - You need to catch visual regressions across multiple pages/locales - The site has known issues with spacing, typography, or broken images - A client/designer has given vague feedback like "it looks off" ## User's Audit Prompt Format (preferred) When the user provides their own audit format/style, prefer it over the defaults. The user has explicitly provided this structured framework: ``` Rol: Senior UX/UI Auditor y Frontend Engineer con enfoque en conversión y estética. Objetivo: Identificar inconsistencias visuales, errores de jerarquía, desperdicio de espacio, falta de balance estético y fricciones de usabilidad. Criterios: - Espaciado y Composición: white space intencional? Márgenes/paddings inconsistentes? - Jerarquía Visual: Qué es lo primero que se ve? Si todo resalta, nada resalta. - Consistencia de Diseño: Botones, inputs, tipografías cambian entre pantallas? Variaciones de 1px? - UX Funcional: Flujos lógicos? Elementos que parecen botones pero no lo son? - Aesthetic Cleanliness: Profesional y moderno o plantilla vieja/desordenada? Formato de salida por pantalla: 🟢 [Nombre/ID] Crítica Estética: (Qué se ve feo o poco profesional) Falla de Eficiencia: (Uso de espacio, redundancias, pasos extra) Inconsistencia Detectada: (Comparado con estándares o pantallas anteriores) Acción Inmediata: (Cambio puntual para arreglar el frontend) ``` ## Detection Trigger ``` Rol: Actúa como un Senior UX/UI Auditor y Frontend Engineer con enfoque en conversión y estética de alto nivel. Objetivo: Identificar inconsistencias visuales, errores de jerarquía, desperdicio de espacio, falta de balance estético y fricciones de usabilidad. No busques lo que está "bien"; busca lo que es mediocre o mejorable. ``` ## Audit Criteria (Framework de Crítica) ### 1. Spacing & Composition - Is white space intentional or dead space? - Are margins/paddings consistent across sections? - Look for `py-16` vs `py-24` alternating without pattern — check via `Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('section')).map(s => window.getComputedStyle(s).paddingTop)` - Any section that dominates scroll (3000px+ for one section) - **CRITICAL: py-24 vs py-20 inconsistency** — when multiple section components hardcode their own padding, the inconsistency is invisible in individual component files. Detection: `Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('section')).map(s => window.getComputedStyle(s).paddingTop)` reveals the pattern. Global fix: change to `py-20` across ALL section source AND dist files simultaneously; partial patching creates more inconsistency. ### 2. Visual Hierarchy - What's the first thing you see? If everything stands out, nothing stands out. - H1 should be 2-3x body text size. Check with: ```js const h1 = window.getComputedStyle(document.querySelector('h1')) const body = window.getComputedStyle(document.body) console.log('H1:', h1.fontSize, 'Body:', body.fontSize) ``` - H2 should be discernibly smaller than H1, larger than body - Font weight range: 400 (body) → 700 (headings) is fine; all-bold is a red flag ### 3. Consistency - Do buttons change size/shape/color between pages? - Are heading tags used semantically? (h1→h2→h3, not h1→h4→h2) - Check letter-spacing on uppercase headings — 0.5-1px is good, 0px is crowded - **CTA consistency:** Compare nav CTA vs hero CTA vs section CTAs. Same text, same href? Different sizes? Different border-radius (50px vs 9999px)? ### 4. Color & Contrast - Gold text on white background — likely fails WCAG AA (ratio < 4.5:1) - Primary CTAs should be highest contrast; secondary CTAs need a border/outline - Background alternation: sections should visually distinguish (bg-white vs bg-surface) - Check `bg-surface-alt` against white — they may be indistinguishable - **CRITICAL: invisible secondary CTA** — transparent button (bg: rgba(0,0,0,0)) with white text on a dark semi-transparent gradient overlay. Detect: check if secondary CTAs have `border: none` or `border-width: 0`. Fix: add `border: 2px solid white/70` or similar. ### 5. Images & Icons **Broken image detection:** ```js // Phase 1: Check all tags document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth === 0 && img.complete) console.log('BROKEN:', img.getAttribute('src')) if (img.naturalWidth === 0 && img.naturalHeight === 0) console.log('EMPTY:', img.getAttribute('src')) }) // Phase 2: Check images loaded but rendering at wrong dimensions document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth > 0 && img.offsetWidth > 0 && img.offsetHeight === 0) { console.log('ZERO-HEIGHT:', img.getAttribute('src'), 'natural:', img.naturalWidth, img.naturalHeight) } }) ``` - Images at 0 height with non-zero width = `complete: false` (file missing, or cookie consent blocking) - **Cookie consent blocks image loading** — images detect as broken on first visit but load after accepting cookies. Always accept cookies BEFORE running image audit. - Team photos that are clearly AI-generated (uncanny valley) — flag as placeholder - Icon families: if using Lucide, all icons must be from the same set - **Full-page screenshots hide images behind cookie consent** — the first-visit cookie banner blocks page content. Use `?r=random` param and accept cookies in the audit script. ### 6. Locale Integrity - Check for Spanish text in NL/EN/DE pages (common: FAQ questions, "En resumen", "¿...?" questions) - Check CTA paths — multi-tenant legacy paths (`/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/`) break navigation - Check that all 4 locales have content for the same page types - **CRITICAL: page-specific content leaking across locales** — NL page showing Spanish FAQ "¿Cuántos viajes?". Check with: ```js document.body.innerText.match(/[¿¡][^?]*\?/g)?.forEach(q => { if (q.includes('¿')) console.log('SPANISH DETECTED in NL/EN:', q.slice(0,40)); }); ``` ### 7. Functional UX - CTA paths: are they correct or redirecting to cookie consent? - Duplicate content: same section appearing on homepage AND dedicated slug page - Hidden content: pages that render only header+footer (slug page but no content data) - Empty pages: pages with just an H1 and no body sections - **Dead pages:** pages the client asked to remove that still exist in sitemap/nexa-pages ## Output Format ### Per-page report (one paragraph each): ``` 🟢 [locale/slug] Crítica Estética: [1-2 sentences on visual defect] Falla de Eficiencia: [space waste, redundancy] Inconsistencia Detectada: [vs other pages or standards] Acción Inmediata: [concrete CSS/content fix command] ``` ### Pattern Summary table (end of report): ``` | Pattern | Severity | Occurrences | |---------|----------|-------------| | H1 same as body text | 🔴 | 1 locale × 1 page | | Broken images | 🔴 | 8 files in whyParaguay | ``` ### Severity scale: - **🔴 Alta** — visible defect on first load (broken images, invisible CTAs, 16px H1) - **🟡 Media** — inconsistency or inefficiency (mixed padding, locale leak, placeholder photos) - **🟢 Baja** — subtle (contrast ratio borderline, minor content duplication) ## Two-Phase Audit Method ### Phase A: Live DOM Analysis (Browser Console) Run these checks in order. MUST accept cookie consent first or results will be misleading: ```js // 0. ACCEPT COOKIES FIRST — find the accept button and click it // document.querySelector('button') that contains 'Accepteren'/'Accept'/'Aceptar' // 1. Section spacing audit const sections = document.querySelectorAll('section'); Array.from(sections).map((s, i) => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(s); return { idx: i, id: s.id || '(none)', h: s.offsetHeight, pt: st.paddingTop, pb: st.paddingBottom, title: (s.querySelector('h1,h2,h3,h4')?.textContent || s.textContent).trim().slice(0,40) }; }); // 2. Typography audit ['h1','h2','h3','h4'].forEach(t => { document.querySelectorAll(t).forEach(el => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(el); console.log(t, st.fontSize, st.fontWeight, st.color, el.textContent.trim().slice(0,30)); }); }); // 3. Image audit document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth === 0 || img.naturalHeight === 0) { console.log('BROKEN/EMPTY:', img.getAttribute('src'), img.alt); } }); // 4. CTA path audit document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="/s/"]').forEach(a => { console.log('LEGACY PATH:', a.textContent.trim().slice(0,30), a.getAttribute('href')); }); // 5. Locale leak check document.body.innerText.match(/[¿¡][^?]*\?/g)?.forEach(q => { if (q.includes('¿')) console.log('SPANISH DETECTED in non-ES:', q.slice(0,40)); }); // 6. Button style consistency const btns = document.querySelectorAll('a, button'); Array.from(btns) .filter(el => el.offsetHeight > 30 && el.offsetWidth > 50) .map(el => ({ text: el.textContent.trim().slice(0,30), w: el.offsetWidth, h: el.offsetHeight, bg: window.getComputedStyle(el).backgroundColor, color: window.getComputedStyle(el).color, radius: window.getComputedStyle(el).borderRadius })); ``` ### Phase B: Screenshot Cross-Reference After capturing screenshots (see `nextjs-docker-swarm` → `references/screenshot-audit-pattern.md`): 1. Sort by file size: `ls -lhS screenshots/full/` 2. Files < 50KB = broken/empty pages — investigate immediately 3. Files of equal exact size (~243KB) = template rendering without data (DE locale common) 4. Files > 1MB = pages with heavy hero images — verify they're real, not AI placeholders ### Post-Content-Cleanup Screenshot Verification After content cleanup (path fixes, locale fixes, page removals), always regenerate screenshots: 1. Delete old: `rm -rf screenshots/full/*.png screenshots/*.png` 2. Regenerate all: run batch script for 4 locales × all slugs 3. Count check: `ls screenshots/full/*.png | wc -l` should match `(slugs - removed_pages) × 4` 4. Size anomaly check: `ls -lhS screenshots/full/ | head -10` — no `< 50KB` unless expected (e.g. cookie legal pages) 5. Git-track: commit fresh screenshots alongside content changes **Slug count verification:** After removing a page (e.g. programas), the Next.js build output should show fewer `[slug]` paths. Compare: - Before: `└ [+101 more paths]` - After: `└ [+97 more paths]` (4 fewer = one slug × 4 locales) ## Critical Patterns from Production These are verified issues from a production 4-locale content site: ### Grid Collapse — Tailwind v4 Cannot Parse `repeat()` in `grid-cols-[...]` **Symptom:** A CSS Grid with `grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))]` renders ALL items stacked in a single column (each card is 100% width = 1120px instead of 268px). The grid container shows a single column layout. **Root cause:** Identical to the H1 clamp() bug — Tailwind v4's arbitrary value parser (`grid-cols-[...]`) interprets commas inside the `repeat()` CSS function as value separators. `grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))]` has commas that break parsing. The rule silently fails — no CSS is generated, no error is thrown. The grid container gets no `grid-template-columns` property, and falls back to block layout (cards stack vertically). **Detection:** ```js // Check if the grid class actually generates CSS let found = false; for (const sheet of document.styleSheets) { try { for (const rule of sheet.cssRules) { if (rule.cssText?.includes('auto-fit') && rule.cssText?.includes('minmax')) found = true; } } catch(e) {} } console.log('Grid CSS rule found:', found); // false = broken ``` Check computed card widths: ```js const grid = document.querySelector('[class*=grid]'); console.log('Grid width:', grid?.offsetWidth, '1st card:', grid?.firstElementChild?.offsetWidth); // If both are the same, grid is collapsed ``` **Fix — Must use a named CSS utility class instead of arbitrary value:** ```css /* In globals.css (or any regular CSS file, NOT in Tailwind arbitrary values) */ @media (min-width: 640px) { .grid-cols-features { grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(240px, 1fr)); } .grid-cols-services { grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)); } } ``` Then in the component JSX: ```tsx // BEFORE (broken): className="grid grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))] gap-6" // AFTER (works): className="grid grid-cols-features gap-4" ``` **Why this works:** Regular CSS files (`globals.css`) use `@import "tailwindcss"` and Tailwind v4 processes `@media` blocks natively. The `repeat()` function is parsed correctly because it's in standard CSS syntax, not inside an arbitrary value bracket. **Applies to ALL grid layout patterns:** `repeat(auto-fit,minmax(...))` for any card grid layout — trust cards, service cards, process steps, team members, gallery, etc. **Metrics fix (nexa-paraguay live):** - Section height: 3,189px → 602px (-81%) - Card images: 1088×607 → 236×132 (-96%) - Total page: 8,973px → 5,122px (-43%) ### H1 Same Size as Body — Tailwind v4 Arbitrary Value Function Bug **Symptom:** `

` uses `font-size: 16px` — identical to `body`. The heading CSS `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` renders at 16px despite the clamp parameters. **⚠️ Critical update:** The Google Fonts `Playfair Display` import at the top of the HTML provides `font-family: 'Playfair Display'` for H1 via the `font-playfair` class. If `font-playfair` fails to load (slow network, blocking), the H1 falls back to sans-serif which can subtly change the perceived size. This is a separate issue from the clamp() bug — verify by checking actual computed font-size values, not just font-family. **Root cause:** Tailwind v4's arbitrary value parser (`text-[...]`) interprets commas inside CSS functions (`clamp()`, `min()`, `max()`, etc.) as value separators. `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` is parsed as two separate values: `clamp(1.8rem` and `4vw,2.8rem)`. Neither compiles. The rule silently fails — no CSS is generated, no error is thrown. The heading gets the browser's default font-size (16px for h1 in many contexts). **Detection:** ```js // Compare heading computed size to clamp parameters const h1 = document.querySelector('h1'); const fs = parseInt(window.getComputedStyle(h1).fontSize); // Expected: ~44px at 1440px viewport (4vw = 57px, clamp to 44.8px max) // Actual: 16px = FAIL console.log('H1 font-size:', fs, 'Expected: ~40-50'); ``` **Fix (two approaches, pick based on how your CSS is structured):** **Approach A — Global CSS override (best for monorepo packages where dist/ is hard to patch):** Add to `src/app/globals.css`: ```css h1 { font-size: clamp(2rem, 5vw, 3.5rem) !important; } h2 { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem) !important; } h3 { font-size: clamp(1.1rem, 2vw, 1.5rem) !important; } ``` The `!important` overrides Tailwind's class-level specificity. This works because `globals.css` is imported in the root layout and applies globally. The Tailwind class `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` still exists in the HTML but is overridden by the global rule. **Approach B — Fix the source dist/ files:** Replace commas with underscores inside arbitrary-value CSS functions: ``` BEFORE: text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)] AFTER: text-[clamp(1.8rem_4vw_2.8rem)] ``` Underscores in arbitrary values are compiled back to spaces/commas correctly by Tailwind v4. The CSS output is identical. **Pitfall with Approach B (tarball-based packages):** The tarball must be REBUILT after the sed fix. The old tarball's SHA is cached in npm's `integrity` field and `package-lock.json`. Every rebuild produces a new tarball SHA. The `package-lock.json` must be regenerated. If you forget, `npm install` in Docker fails with `integrity checksum failed`. Fix: `rm -rf /root/.npm/_cacache package-lock.json && npm install --legacy-peer-deps` before Docker build. **Pitfall with Approach B (Docker build):** The `.dockerignore` excludes `node_modules/`. If you patched the dist/ files in `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/*.js` on the host but the Docker build uses the tarball from `.packages/`, the patches are ignored. Always rebuild the tarball AFTER patching: ```bash cd node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections npm pack --pack-destination /root/nexa-paraguay/.packages/ ``` **Applies to ALL CSS functions:** `clamp()`, `min()`, `max()`, `calc()`, `rgb()`, `hsl()`, `linear-gradient()`, `url()`, etc. — any function with commas inside `text-[...]`, `w-[...]`, `h-[...]`, `bg-[...]`, etc. ### Exit Popup Timing — Don't Trigger Too Early When auditing a site with exit-intent lead-gen popups, check for overly aggressive timing: - **Idle timer:** 45s minimum (30s is too early for first visit) - **Exit intent:** `e.clientY <= 5` must respect `dismissed` flag - **localStorage:** Form submission + dismissal must persist across sessions - **Re-trigger prevention:** Once dismissed, `setTimeout` callback must NOT re-show **Detection:** ```bash grep -n "setTimeout\|mouseleave\|localStorage" src/components/ExitPopup.tsx # Check: 30000 = too short (change to 45000) # Check: dismissed check in both the setTimeout AND mouseleave handlers ``` **Detection in built CSS:** Search the compiled CSS for both the broken and fixed versions. If both exist as separate rules, the old code is still in the bundle: ```bash docker exec grep -o 'text-\[clamp[^]]*\]' /app/.next/static/chunks/main-*.js | sort -u ``` Comma version = old/broken. Underscore version = fixed. ### Missing Image Files (8 Broken in One Page) **Symptom:** 8 of 11 images on a page have `naturalWidth: 0, complete: false` in DOM but files exist on disk. **Root cause:** Cookie consent banner blocks image loading on first visit. Images register as broken until cookies are accepted. ALWAYS accept cookies before the image audit phase. **When images are genuinely missing:** files referenced in `images.json`/content JSON don't exist at the filesystem path. Detect: ```bash python3 -c " import json import os with open('images.json') as f: img = json.load(f) img['images']['whyParaguay'] # check the specific section " ls -la public/images/why-paraguay/ ``` **Fix:** Create placeholder SVGs: ```bash for name in tax growth agribusiness community nature culture; do cat > "public/images/why-paraguay/${name}.svg" << EOF EOF done ``` Then update `images.json` to add `srcSvg` entries. ### Team Photos — Some Missing **Symptom:** Team section renders heading + description for all members, but only first N have `` tags. **Root cause:** Content JSON has image references (`imageUrl`/`memberImage`) for only the first members. Either: - The images.json entry is missing for the last members - The image files exist but the src path is incorrect - The content JSON only has entries for the first members **Fix:** Check images.json first, then check public/images/team/, then check content JSON. Add placeholder SVGs or change component to render initials as fallback when no image is found. ### Inconsistent Section Padding **Symptom:** Sections alternate `py-16` (64px) and `py-24` (96px) without pattern. Visual rhythm is broken. **Root cause:** Each section component hardcodes its own padding rather than inheriting from a layout wrapper. The inconsistency spans 8+ component files. **Fix:** Change ALL section `py-24` → `py-20` AND `py-16` → `py-20` simultaneously in both `src/` AND `dist/` files (both are resolved by the bundler). **⚠️ When fixing shared packages (monorepo):** The `dist/` files are what the bundler actually uses at runtime. Editing only `src/` (TypeScript) does NOT fix the built CSS — the `dist/` still has the old values. Both must be updated: ```bash # Fix src/ sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' src/*.tsx sed -i 's/py-16/py-20/g' src/*.tsx # Fix dist/ (what actually gets bundled) sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' dist/*.js sed -i 's/py-16/py-20/g' dist/*.js ``` ### Invisible Secondary CTA **Symptom:** Secondary CTA (e.g. "Bekijk diensten") has `bg: transparent, color: white, border: none` on a gradient hero background. Invisible to the user. **Detect:** ```js document.querySelectorAll('a').forEach(a => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(a); if (st.backgroundColor === 'rgba(0, 0, 0, 0)' && parseInt(st.borderWidth) === 0) { console.log('TRANSPARENT:', a.textContent.trim().slice(0,30)); } }); ``` **Fix:** `border: 2px solid white/70` or add a subtle background tint. ### Footer Gold-on-White Contrast Fail **Symptom:** Footer headings in gold (`#C9A96E` or `oklab(0.68 0.026 0.08)`) on white background. ~2.8:1 contrast ratio — fails WCAG AA (needs 4.5:1 for text < 18px). **Detect:** Check the `bg-primary` footer for gold text: ```js document.querySelectorAll('footer h4, footer h3').forEach(h => { const bg = window.getComputedStyle(h.closest('footer')).backgroundColor; const textColor = window.getComputedStyle(h).color; console.log(h.textContent, 'on', bg, 'text:', textColor); }); ``` **Fix:** Change to `text-white` on `bg-primary` (navy), which achieves ~12:1 contrast. ### CTA Consistency Audit When multiple CTAs exist across sections (nav, hero, sections, footer), they must be visually and functionally consistent: **Check text alignment:** Same label for the same action? `Plan gratis consult` in nav but `Plan uw gratis consult` in hero should use the same label. **Check href consistency:** Both point to the same URL? Detect: ```js document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="contacto"], a[href*="gratis"]').forEach(a => { console.log(a.textContent.trim().slice(0,25), '→', a.getAttribute('href')); }); ``` **Check visual:** Same border-radius, padding, font-size, background? Nav CTA with 50px radius ≠ hero CTA with 9999px radius is a visual inconsistency. **Audit function:** look for at least 5 CTA variants — button, link, card, underlined text, icon — and flag if they exceed 3 distinct styles. ### Multi-Tenant Path Carryover **Symptom:** CTAs point to `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` instead of `/nl/contacto`. These redirect to cookie consent page with blank content. **Detection:** `document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="/s/"]').length` or `grep -r '/s/[a-z]\{2\}/nexa-paraguay' content/*.json | wc -l` **Fix:** Search-and-replace across ALL content JSON files: ```bash grep -r '/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/' content/nl.json | wc -l # Then for each locale: sed -i 's|/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/|/nl/|g' content/nl.json sed -i 's|/s/en/nexa-paraguay/|/en/|g' content/en.json # etc. ``` Also fix bare `/servicios` links (missing locale prefix): `sed -i 's|"/servicios"|"/nl/servicios"|g' content/nl.json` ### Page Removal from Sitemap When the client asks to remove a full page (e.g., "Programas"): 1. Delete `nexa-pages/programas.json` — this removes the route 2. Remove the navigation entry from ALL 4 locale content files 3. Remove from `site.json` navigation array 4. Update any CTA hrefs that pointed to it 5. Remove the `programasPage` key from `types.ts` 6. Remove any `"programasPage": ""` empty placeholder entries (they become dead code) 7. Rebuild: fewer `[slug]` paths in build output confirms removal 8. Screenshot: old page should show header+footer only (empty slug) ## Automated Screenshot + Audit Pipeline For full integration with a deploy workflow: 1. Deploy 2. Wait for convergence: `docker service ps --format '{{.CurrentState}}' | head -1 | grep -q Running` 3. Run screenshot batch: see `nextjs-docker-swarm` → `references/screenshot-audit-pattern.md` 4. Run DOM audit on 3-4 key pages (homepage, services, about, blog) 5. Generate report with findings + screenshot file size anomalies 6. Commit screenshots + report to repo **Pitfalls** - **Vision API fails on full-page screenshots** — most AI vision tools can't process 1.6MB+ screenshots reliably. Use DOM analysis + console scripts instead. - **Cookies block content on first visit** — the cookie consent banner appears on every new session. Screenshots and DOM checks must accept cookies first. Use `?r=random` cachebuster to get a fresh page load. - **Blog list pages appear empty** — they render article previews via client components. Not a visual defect but looks like one. Note as "expected behavior." - **PPR (Partial Prerendering)** — static HTML shell with empty `
`. Content serialized in RSC script payloads. Always verify via headless browser, not curl. - **DE locale often has minimal content** — the least-complete locale. 44KB screenshots vs 1.6MB for NL = DE page template rendered without data. - **Screenshot size uniformity ≠ quality** — if ALL pages are exactly 243KB, template rendered without data. Check content JSON for that locale. - **Tailwind v4 arbitrary-value function syntax** — commas break CSS functions in `text-[...]`, `w-[...]`, etc. Use underscores. This fail produces NO error, NO CSS rule, and NO devtools hint. The only symptom is the heading renders at browser default size. - **CSS function in arbitrary values is invisible in devtools** — when `text-[clamp(...)]` fails, no CSS rule appears. Detection: compare computed font-size vs expected `clamp()` output. - **`py-24` vs `py-20` inconsistency invisible in component-level review** — only manifests visually on the rendered page. Always verify section padding with `getComputedStyle()` on the live site. - **Docker build copies HOST node_modules** — if `.dockerignore` doesn't exclude `node_modules/`, `COPY . .` overwrites the freshly-installed node_modules with whatever's on the host. Always verify `.dockerignore` has `node_modules/`. ## E-commerce Full Audit (CRO + Code + Performance) When the user asks to "analyze and roast" or "critique" an e-commerce site, the visual audit above is necessary but not sufficient. A full audit combines DOM analysis with codebase inspection, CRO methodology, and performance profiling. ### Audit Layers (run in order) **Layer 1: Browser Accessibility Snapshot** — `browser_snapshot(full=true)` gives you the complete DOM tree. From this alone you can assess: - Navigation structure and label quality - Form accessibility (labels, placeholders, required attributes) - Heading hierarchy (h1→h2→h3 consistency) - CTA text and placement - Trust signals present or absent **Layer 2: Codebase Analysis** — Read the actual page component, data sources, and checkout flow: - `app/tienda/page.tsx` → main catalog page - `components/search-filters.tsx` → filter logic (watch for missing useMemo deps) - `app/checkout/page.tsx` → payment flow (watch for hardcoded numbers) - `content/*.json` → product data (watch for prices stored as formatted strings) - `app/globals.css` → theme/design tokens **Layer 3: Performance** — Check image pipeline: ```bash find public/images -name '*.png' -o -name '*.jpg' | xargs du -ch | tail -1 # total size find public/images -name '*.webp' | wc -l # should be > 0 ``` Flag: >1MB per product image = critical. >100MB total images = critical for mobile users. **Layer 4: SEO** — Check for `generateMetadata` on key pages: ```bash grep -rn 'metadata\|generateMetadata' app/tienda/ app/producto/ 2>/dev/null ``` Flag: no metadata exports on catalog/product pages = invisible to search engines. **Layer 5: CRO Framework** — Apply objection/counter-objection analysis: - Is the value proposition clear in 5 seconds? - Are trust signals (reviews, testimonials, guarantees) visible? - Is there urgency/scarcity? - Is the checkout flow actually functional or just a WhatsApp redirect? ### React Anti-Patterns to Check During Audit | Pattern | Why It's Bad | Detection | |---------|-------------|-----------| | `setTimeout(() => setState(x), 0)` parent callback | Papering over infinite re-render bugs; causes flash of stale content | `grep -n 'setTimeout.*onFiltered\|setTimeout.*set' component.tsx` | | Missing `useMemo` deps | Filters silently fail to update | Check the dep array against all state variables used inside | | Double context provider wrapping | Inner state isolated from rest of app | Check root layout + page-level providers for duplicates | | `supabase` created inside component body without memo | New client instance every render | Should be `useMemo` or module-level | | `slugify` duplicated across files | DRY violation, drift risk | `grep -rn 'function slugify' --include='*.tsx'` | | Prices stored as formatted strings (`"Gs. 450.000"`) | Re-parsed everywhere with regex; fragile | Check content JSON for price field types | ### E-commerce Trust Signals Checklist For a Paraguayan PYME e-commerce site, check these specifically: - [ ] Real brand names (not AI-generated compounds like "FishMaster", "SleepWell") - [ ] Physical address visible (not just in footer/legal) - [ ] Phone/WhatsApp number consistent across all files (not hardcoded in 5 places) - [ ] Customer reviews or testimonials on catalog pages - [ ] Guarantee/return policy visible before checkout - [ ] Shipping costs shown early (not surprise at checkout) - [ ] Payment methods clearly listed with icons - [ ] Stock indicators that create urgency (not just "NUEVO" on everything) ## Session References For real-world applications of this audit framework, see: - `references/nexa-paraguay-ux-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md` — 4-locale visual audit + content cleanup - `references/elviajero-tienda-cro-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md` — full e-commerce CRO + code teardown ", "path": "development/visual-ui-audit/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/development/visual-ui-audit", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/cta-section-content-resolution-trap.md", "references/elviajero-tienda-cro-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/layout-compression-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/nexa-paraguay-ux-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available"}

{"success": true, "name": "voice-ai-infrastructure", "description": "Deploy, configure, and operate real-time voice AI infrastructure — LiveKit WebRTC server, voice agents (STT→LLM→TTS), and meeting participation bots (AgentCall) for AI-to-human voice conversations. Covers Docker networking, API key provisioning, DNS setup for WebRTC, and integration with existing Hermes infrastructure.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: voice-ai-infrastructure
description: Deploy, configure, and operate real-time voice AI infrastructure — LiveKit WebRTC server, voice agents (STT→LLM→TTS), and meeting participation bots (AgentCall) for AI-to-human voice conversations. Covers Docker networking, API key provisioning, DNS setup for WebRTC, and integration with existing Hermes infrastructure.
version: 1.1.0  # Updated with pricing analysis
author: erebus
license: Apache 2.0
---

# Voice AI Infrastructure

Class-level skill for deploying real-time voice AI: LiveKit (WebRTC server + voice agents) and AgentCall (AI meeting participant). Together they enable voice conversations between humans and AI agents — from one-on-one chat to multi-party meeting participation.

## Architecture

```
Your Device (mic + speaker)
    │
    ├── WebRTC ── LiveKit Server (:7880)
    │                 │
    │                 ├── Hermes Voice Agent (STT→LLM→TTS)
    │                 │     ├── Deepgram (STT)
    │                 │     ├── ElevenLabs (TTS)
    │                 │     └── OpenAI Realtime (LLM)
    │                 │
    │                 └── (future: more agents)
    │
    └── Browser ── AgentCall MCP (:stdio)
                      │
                      ├── Join Google Meet/Zoom/Teams
                      ├── Speak/listen/interrupt
                      └── Base $0.35/hr (TTS add-on $8/hr)
```

## Components

| Component | Purpose | Provider | Cost | Self-Hostable |
|-----------|---------|----------|------|---------------|
| **LiveKit Server** | WebRTC signaling, room management, TURN | Open source (Apache 2.0) | Free | ✅ Yes |
| **Hermes Voice Agent** | STT→LLM→TTS pipeline | Python + LiveKit SDK | Free | ✅ Yes |
| **Deepgram** | Speech-to-text (nova-3 model) | Cloud API | $0.46/hr or $200 free credit | ❌ No |
| **ElevenLabs** | Text-to-speech (multilingual v2) | Cloud API | ~$0.15/hr (10k chars/mo free) | ❌ No |
| **AgentCall** | AI participant in meetings | Cloud service | $0.35/hr base | ❌ No |
| **[Planned] Self-Hosted Pipeline** | Vexa + Deepgram + ElevenLabs | Your infra | ~$0.61/hr | ✅ |

## Pricing Comparison: Which Service Covers Everything?

**User preference (Ivan):** "I'd rather pay only 1 subscription" — wants a single service that covers bot meeting join + STT + TTS.

### The Short Answer

**No single subscription covers all three.** Here's why:

| If you subscribe to... | It covers | Missing |
|------------------------|-----------|---------|
| **ElevenLabs Pro $99/mo** | STT + TTS + AI agent + 100 chars/mo | ❌ Can't join Google Meet |
| **Deepgram $200 credit** | STT only (~430 free hours) | ❌ No TTS, no meeting join |
| **AgentCall Base $0.35/hr** | Meeting join + listen | ❌ No TTS (+$8/hr extra) |
| **AgentCall Full $8.47/hr** | Everything | ✅ But expensive |

### Service Coverage Matrix

| Capability | Deepgram | ElevenLabs | AgentCall |
|-----------|----------|------------|-----------|
| **STT** (listen to speech) | ✅ Best-in-class (nova-3) | ✅ Scribe (competitive model) | ❌ Uses Deepgram under the hood |
| **TTS** (speak back) | ❌ | ✅ World's best quality | ✅ Kokoro (basic, open-source) |
| **Bot joins Google Meet** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Only service that does this |
| **Bot joins Zoom/Teams** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Only service that does this |
| **Conversational AI agent** | ❌ | ✅ ElevenAgents (full platform, $6/hr) | ✅ Bridge + MCP tools |
| **Screenshare** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Included | 
| **Meeting chat** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Bidirectional |

### The AgentCall TTS Problem

AgentCall charges **$8/hr** for TTS. The TTS engine is **Kokoro** — an open-source model that runs on CPU. Compare:

| Service | Hourly Cost | Quality |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **AgentCall TTS** | **$8.00** | Kokoro (basic, open-source) |
| **ElevenLabs Flash** | **~$0.15** | Best-in-world, $0.05/1K chars |
| **ElevenLabs Multilingual v2** | **~$0.30** | Premium, $0.10/1K chars |

AgentCall TTS is **53x more expensive** than ElevenLabs for lower quality. Never pay for AgentCall TTS.

### Cost Per Hour — Meeting Bot That Talks

| Strategy | Breakdown | Total/hr | Notes |
|----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| **AgentCall Full** | Base $0.35 + TTS add-on $8.00 | **$8.47** | ✅ Everything, but waste of money |
| **AgentCall Base + ElevenLabs** | Base $0.35 + ElevenLabs $0.15 | **$0.50** | ✅ Best value — use ElevenLabs for TTS |
| **Deepgram + ElevenLabs + Vexa** | STT $0.46 + TTS $0.15, join is free | **$0.61** | ✅ Fully self-hosted (WIP — needs audio relay) |
| **ElevenAgents** | $0.10/min = $6/hr | **$6.00** | STT+TTS+LLM all-in-one, no meeting join |

### Recommended Architecture

**Publishable as the "default recommendation" for this user:**

```
1. Bot joins meeting → AgentCall Base ($0.35/hr) OR Vexa (free)
2. Hears client → Deepgram STT ($0.46/hr, often free with credits)
3. Decides response → Your LLM (free via Groq/Hermes)
4. Speaks back → ElevenLabs TTS ($0.15/hr)
```

**Total operating cost: $0.50-$0.61/hr** — versus $8.47/hr for AgentCall's full stack. The saving comes from replacing AgentCall's marked-up TTS with ElevenLabs' direct API.

**If you want a truly single-subscription experience with no assembly required:** AgentCall Full at $8.47/hr works, but you pay for the convenience of not integrating ElevenLabs yourself. Given you already have both API keys set up, the integration is ~2-3 hours of work.

## LiveKit Server Deployment

### Docker Compose (Standalone, Behind Traefik)

```yaml
name: livekit

services:
  livekit-server:
    image: livekit/livekit-server:latest
    environment:
      LIVEKIT_KEYS: "api-key: api-secret"
      LIVEKIT_REDIS_HOST: "redis:6379"
      LIVEKIT_PORT: "7880"
      LIVEKIT_UDP_PORT: "7881"
      LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_START: "7882"
      LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_END: "7890"
      LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED: "false"
      LIVEKIT_LOGGING_LEVEL: "info"
    ports:
      - "7880:7880"
      - "7881:7881/udp"
      - "[REDACTED_PHONE]:[REDACTED_PHONE]/udp"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.rule=Host(`livekit.paragu-ai.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.tls=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
      - "traefik.http.services.livekit-api.loadbalancer.server.port=7880"

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    command: redis-server --appendonly yes --save 60 1
    networks:
      - agent-net
```

### Networking Notes

- **WebRTC needs direct UDP** — Cloudflare proxied DNS (orange cloud) will NOT work for voice. The `livekit` A record MUST be DNS-only (gray cloud).
- The server exposes ports 7880 (HTTP/WS), 7881 (UDP ICE), and [REDACTED_PHONE] (RTC).
- TURN requires a valid TLS cert — disable with `LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED=false` for testing without one.

### Verify Server is Running

```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:7880/
# Expected: OK
```

The root `/` returns `OK` — no JSON, no HTML. This is normal. The WebSocket signaling endpoint is at `/rtc`.

## Voice Agent (Python, LiveKit Agents SDK v1.5+)

The Hermes voice agent is a Python program using `livekit-agents` SDK. It connects to the LiveKit server, listens for room sessions, and runs the STT→LLM→TTS loop.

### Agent Script Structure

```python
import os
from livekit.agents import Agent, AgentSession, JobContext, WorkerOptions, cli, function_tool
from livekit.plugins import silero, deepgram, elevenlabs, openai

DG_KEY = os.environ.get("DEEPGRAM_API_KEY", "")
EL_KEY = os.environ.get("ELEVENLABS_API_KEY", "")

@function_tool
async def ask_hermes(context: RunContext, message: str):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120) as client:
        resp = await client.post("http://hermes:8080/chat", json={"message": message})
        return resp.json().get("response", "Got a response.")

async def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext):
    session = AgentSession(
        vad=silero.VAD.load(),
        stt=deepgram.STT(model="nova-2-general", language="en", api_key=DG_KEY),
        tts=elevenlabs.TTS(model="eleven_multilingual_v2", voice="21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM", api_key=EL_KEY),
        llm=openai.realtime.RealtimeModel(model="gpt-4o-realtime-preview", voice="alloy"),
        turn_detection=openai.realtime.TurnDetector(),
    )
    agent = Agent(instructions="...", tools=[ask_hermes])
    await session.start(agent=agent, room=ctx.room)
    await session.generate_reply(instructions="Greet the user...")
    await session.wait_for_end()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cli.run_app(WorkerOptions(entrypoint_fnc=entrypoint))
```

### Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir \
    livekit-agents[deepgram,elevenlabs,silero,openai]~=1.5 \
    httpx
COPY agent.py .
CMD ["python", "agent.py"]
```

### API VERSION NOTES (CRITICAL)

**LiveKit Agents SDK v1.5+ changed the API:**

| Old API (v1.4) | New API (v1.5+) | Notes |
|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| `AgentServer(entrypoint_fnc=fn)` | `cli.run_app(WorkerOptions(entrypoint_fnc=fn))` | Server object removed |
| `@server.rtc_session()` decorator | No decorator | Just pass function directly |
| `from livekit.agents import AgentServer` | `from livekit.agents import WorkerOptions` | Import changes |

**Pitfall:** Attempting to use `AgentServer(entrypoint_fnc=fn)` with v1.5+ gives:
```
TypeError: AgentServer.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'entrypoint_fnc'
```

### Service Registration

When the agent starts, it logs:
```json
{"message": "registered worker", "agent_name": "", "id": "AW_xxxx"}
```

This means it's connected to the LiveKit server and ready to accept room sessions.

### Cartesia TTS Fallback

If the ElevenLabs API key is not available, fall back to Cartesia (free 30min tier):
```python
tts_provider = cartesia.TTS(model="sonic-2", voice="9626c31c-bec5-4cca-baa8-f8ba9e84c8bc")
```

Requires: `livekit-agents[cartesia]~=1.5`

## AgentCall Setup (AI Meeting Participant)

AgentCall is a commercial service ($0.35/hr base) that lets an AI join Google Meet/Zoom/Teams as a participant. It handles the hard parts: real-time voice, barge-in prevention, interruption handling, screen share detection.

### As MCP Server in Hermes

AgentCall exposes 21 tools via MCP. To connect Hermes to AgentCall:

1. **Get an API key:** https://app.agentcall.dev/api-keys (format: `ak_ac_xxxxxxxx`)
2. **Run the MCP server** as a Docker container:
```yaml
  agentcall-mcp:
    image: node:20-slim
    command: sh -c "npx -y @agentcall/mcp-server"
    environment:
      AGENTCALL_API_KEY: "${AGENTCALL_API_KEY}"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
```
3. **Add to Hermes config.yaml:**
```yaml
env:
  AGENTCALL_API_KEY: ak_ac_xxxx
mcp_servers:
  agentcall:
    transport: stdio
    command: docker
    args:
      - exec
      - "-i"
      - livekit-agentcall-mcp-1
      - npx
      - "-y"
      - "@agentcall/mcp-server"
```

**Note:** The MCP server uses STDIO transport. `docker exec -i` (interactive mode, no TTY) is required.

### AgentCall Bridge Usage — Interactive Session Required

**Critical:** The `bridge.py` script requires interactive stdin to send TTS commands. If you start it with `> logfile 2>&1` or its stdin is `/dev/null`, the bot joins the meeting but **never speaks**. The bot CAN hear participants (transcripts appear on stdout) but CANNOT respond.

**Correct usage (enables speaking):**
```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/agentcall-meet/scripts/python
AGENTCALL_API_KEY=ak_ac_xxxx python3 bridge.py "https://meet.google.com/xxx" --name "Hermes" --voice "en_us_001"
# Type: tts.speak "Hello, this is a test message"
```

**Incorrect usage (bot joins but stays silent):**
```bash
# Process started as background job — stdin = /dev/null
AGENTCALL_API_KEY=ak_ac_xxxx python3 bridge.py "https://meet.google.com/xxx" > log.txt 2>&1 &
# ^^ Bot joins, hears everyone, but never speaks
```

**Stdin check:**
```bash
ls -la /proc/$PID/fd/0
# /dev/null  → bot is deaf-mute (can hear, can't speak)
# /dev/pts/0 → bot can speak (interactive terminal)
```

**Troubleshooting checklist for silent bot:**
1. Check stdin: `ls -la /proc/$PID/fd/0`
2. Check API key prefix: must start with `ac_live_`
3. Check concurrent calls: `GET /v1/calls` — free plan allows 1 at a time
4. Wait ~2s after deleting a stale call before creating a new one
5. Telltale sign: stdout shows `greeting.prompt` / `user.message` events but no speak command is ever sent

### Managing Active Calls (API)

```bash
# List active calls
curl -s 'https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls' -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx'

# End a call (force cleanup)
curl -s -X DELETE "https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}" -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx'

# Send TTS via API
curl -s -X PUT "https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}" \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"action":"set_user_input","text":"tts.speak Hello world"}'

# Direct WebSocket control
ws_url = f"wss://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}/ws?api_key={API_KEY}"
# The WebSocket receives events (transcripts, participant join/leave) and accepts speak commands
```

### Concurrent Call Limit

Free plan: **1 concurrent call**. Attempting a second returns 429. Check before creating.

## LiveKit Config: CLI Flags vs YAML

**v1.11.0 does NOT support YAML config files** — use environment variables only. YAML-based config fails with:
```
could not parse config: yaml: unmarshal errors:
  line 2: field bind not found in type config.Config
```

| Config Item | Env Var | Default |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| Port | `LIVEKIT_PORT` | 7880 |
| UDP port | `LIVEKIT_UDP_PORT` | 7881 |
| RTC port range | `LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_START` / `_END` | [REDACTED_PHONE] |
| Redis | `LIVEKIT_REDIS_HOST` | (none, required) |
| API keys | `LIVEKIT_KEYS` | (none, required) |
| TURN enabled | `LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED` | true |
| TURN domain | `LIVEKIT_TURN_DOMAIN` | (none) |
| Log level | `LIVEKIT_LOGGING_LEVEL` | info |

Use env vars from docker-compose; do NOT mount a config file.

## DNS Setup

`livekit.paragu-ai.com` → A record pointing to VPS IP. **Must be DNS-only (gray cloud).** Cloudflare proxied mode breaks WebRTC UDP connections.

## Pitfalls

### 1. AgentCall MCP Service First Launch
First launch takes ~30s to download dependencies. The container logs:
```
\|/-\\|/-\\\\|/- // spinner // AgentCall MCP server running (21 tools, 4 prompts registered)
```

### 2. API Key Masking in Terminal Output
Use Python to inspect Docker envs when the credential masker hides values:
```bash
docker inspect container | python3 -c "import sys,json; c=json.load(sys.stdin)[0]; print([x for x in c['Config']['Env'] if 'ADMIN' in x])"
```

### 3. Key Length Warning for LiveKit
```
InsecureKeyLengthWarning: The HMAC key is 23 bytes long...
```
Not harmful in dev. Use 32+ bytes for production.

### 4. WebRTC Requires UDP Ports Open
Firewall must allow UDP on ports [REDACTED_PHONE].

### 5. No Built-in LiveKit Test UI
Use `livekit-cli` (`pip install livekit-cli`) to join a room from terminal, or use the LiveKit React SDK in a browser.

### 6. AgentCall TTS Cost
**Never pay for AgentCall's $8/hr TTS add-on.** Use ElevenLabs directly ($0.15/hr). See pricing analysis above.

## Joinly — Open-Source Alternative (Replaces AgentCall Entirely)

[joinly.ai](https://github.com/joinly-ai/joinly) — **493 stars, MIT license.** Single Docker image that joins Google Meet/Zoom/Teams and lets the AI both listen AND speak. Replaces the entire AgentCall + ElevenLabs hybrid with one container.

### Key Advantages Over AgentCall

| | Joinly (open-source) | AgentCall (commercial) |
|--|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Cost** | **$0** (VPS resources) | $0.35/hr base + TTS |
| **STT** | Whisper (local, free) or Deepgram API | Uses Deepgram under hood |
| **TTS** | Kokoro (local, free) or ElevenLabs API | Kokoro at $8/hr markup |
| **LLM** | Any (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, DeepSeek) | Their choice |
| **Joins** | Google Meet, Zoom, Teams | Same |
| **Interruptions** | ✅ Built-in turn detection | ✅ Barge-in prevention |
| **MCP support** | ✅ Full MCP server for code tools | ✅ MCP tools |
| **Docker** | ✅ `docker run` single command | Separate bridge script |
| **License** | MIT (Free) | Proprietary ($0.35/hr) |

### Quick Deploy

```bash
docker run --rm --network host \
  --env-file .env \
  ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest \
  --client "https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij" \
  --name "Hermes" \
  --tts elevenlabs \
  --tts-arg voice_id=21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM \
  --stt deepgram \
  --stt-arg model_name=nova-2-general \
  -v
```

### .env File

```env
# LLM (use any OpenAI-compatible provider — DeepSeek is free)
JOINLY_LLM_MODEL=deepseek-chat
JOINLY_LLM_PROVIDER=openai
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://api.deepseek.com/v1

# STT
DEEPGRAM_API_KEY=your-deepgram-key

# TTS
ELEVENLABS_API_KEY=your-elevenlabs-key
```

### Architecture

```bash
docker run --network host ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest --client <URL>
                                        │
                                        ▼
                          Joinly Container (single process)
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                    │  Headless Chromium (Playwright)  │
                    │  → Joins Google Meet/Zoom/Teams │
                    │  → Captures browser audio       │
                    │  → Plays TTS audio back          │
                    │                                  │
                    │  STT → LLM → TTS Pipeline        │
                    │  (configurable providers)        │
                    │                                  │
                    │  Turn Detection                  │
                    │  → Interruption handling         │
                    │  → Natural conversation flow     │
                    └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Joinly Fields Tested (Live Meeting, May 10 2026)

**Successfully tested in a real Google Meet at `cvh-sprr-mba`.** The bot:
1. ✅ Joined the meeting as "Hermes"
2. ✅ **Spoke first**: "Hi Steve, this is Hermes. I'm here to help out."
3. ✅ Heard the user: "ivan weiss van der pol: I am Ivan."
4. ✅ **Handled interruption**: When interrupted mid-sentence by detected speech, Joinly stopped speaking and waited
5. ✅ **Corrected itself**: "Got it, Ivan! Sorry for the mix-up. How can I help you today?"
6. ✅ Maintained a full conversation flow: the AI asked about client needs, explained its process\n7. ✅ **Responded in real-time** through the meeting speakers\n\n**Pitfall: `--prompt` vs `--system-prompt`.** Joinly uses `--prompt` (not `--system-prompt`). Shell escaping multi-line prompts works but flags are case-sensitive. Using `--system-prompt` gives: `Error: No such option: --system-prompt Did you mean --prompt?`

**Stack used:**
- Joinly Docker image (ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest)
- Deepgram STT (nova-2-general via `--stt deepgram`)
- DeepSeek LLM (free, via `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://api.deepseek.com/v1`)
- ElevenLabs TTS (Rachel voice via `--tts elevenlabs --tts-arg voice_id=21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM`)

**Notable observations from the live test:**
- The greeting was for "Steve" instead of "Ivan" — the LLM hallucinated the name. This is a prompt engineering issue, not a platform limitation.
- **Interruption handling works naturally** — Joinly uses VAD (voice activity detection) and stops speaking when the user starts talking
- The `.env` file must include `OPENAI_BASE_URL` for DeepSeek compatibility (Joinly uses the OpenAI client library)
- Deepgram key, ElevenLabs key, and OpenAI key (or compatible) are required at minimum
- The `--network host` flag is required for PulseAudio virtual microphone to function inside the container
- CPU-only — no GPU needed for this stack (Whisper STT runs on CPU, but Deepgram cloud STT was used instead)

### Known Limitations

- **Speaker diarization** — Joinly detects when speech starts/stops but doesn't always identify WHO is speaking in multi-party meetings. This is a known open issue in the repo (#175).
- **CAPTCHA handling** — Google sometimes blocks automated browser joins with CAPTCHA. Workaround: use a long-lived session cookie or manually admit the bot (works fine with lobby).
- **Best for 1-on-1** — Works well in one-on-one conversations. Multi-party (3+ people) gets confusing for the turn detection.
- **Deepgram WebSocket failure** — Joinly containers fail with `RuntimeError: Client failed to connect: Failed to connect to Deepgram STT service.` even when started clean. This occurs even when Deepgram API key is valid and no other containers are running. **Fix:** Use `--stt google` instead of `--stt deepgram`. However, Google STT requires `GOOGLE_API_KEY` or `GEMINI_API_KEY` env var set. Without it, you get: `GEMINI_API_KEY or GOOGLE_API_KEY must be set in the environment.`. **Alternative:** Use `--stt whisper` — runs local Whisper STT inside the container (no API key needed). The container starts successfully but may not capture audio correctly from headless Chromium without PulseAudio setup. See `references/joinly-deepgram-troubleshooting.md` for full debugging steps.

### Repo Context Injection — Fixing "Bot Doesn't Have Context"

**Problem:** When the user asks the bot about the repo (code, issues, docs), Joinly's LLM (DeepSeek, GPT, etc.) has no internet access and can't browse GitHub. Even with GitHub MCP configured, Joinly's LLM doesn't invoke it autonomously.

**Fix:** Pre-fetch repo contents and inject them into Joinly's `--prompt` before the meeting starts. The `repo_context.py` script clones/pulls the repo and extracts: README, config files (package.json, composer.json, requirements.txt), and file structure.

```bash
python3 /root/vexa-deploy/joinly/repo_context.py "https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio" 2>/dev/null
# Output: README content + structure (266 files) + config files
```

**Integration in `hermes-meeting.sh`:**
```bash
REPO_CONTEXT=$(python3 /root/vexa-deploy/joinly/repo_context.py "$REPO_URL" 2>/dev/null)
SYSTEM_PROMPT="$MEETING_DIR/system-prompt.txt"
cat > "$SYSTEM_PROMPT" << PROMPT_EOF
$AGENT_CONTEXT

REPOSITORY CONTEXT:
$REPO_CONTEXT

[interview protocol...]
PROMPT_EOF
```

This way the bot knows the project structure, README, and key files before anyone asks.

**File:** `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/repo_context.py`

### User Preference: "Implement, Don't Propose"

**Signal:** User asked "what more can we upgrade?" twice. First response listed options. User pushed back: "implement all of this." After that, every request was executed immediately without listing tradeoffs or asking for confirmation.

**Rule:** When Ivan asks "what can we do/upgrade?" → estimate briefly, then build it. Don't provide a menu of options unless specifically asked for comparison. The preferred pattern is:

```
User: "what can we improve?"
You: [1-sentence answer] then start building immediately.
```

### Cron Script Placement — Symlinks Do Not Work

**Problem:** Hermes cron jobs validate that scripts reside in `~/.hermes/scripts/`. Symlinks to files outside that directory pass `ls` checks but fail cron job creation with `"Script path escapes the scripts directory via traversal"`.

**Fix:** Copy the script file into `~/.hermes/scripts/`, do not symlink. The cron system checks the resolved path against the scripts directory.

```bash
cp /path/to/script.py ~/.hermes/scripts/weekly_digest.py
# NOT: ln -sf /path/to/script.py ~/.hermes/scripts/weekly_digest.py
```

This applies to ALL cron scripts, not just this skill's files.

### Full Hermes Meeting Bot Pipeline (May 2026 Build)

```bash
bash /root/vexa-deploy/joinly/hermes-meeting.sh "https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij"
```

**What runs in sequence:**

| Step | Component | File | What it does |
|------|-----------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Context MCP Server | `hermes-mcp-server.py` | Starts a Docker container serving client data, project info, skills, pricing via JSON-RPC |
| 2 | Joinly | Docker image | Joins Google Meet as "Hermes", listens via Deepgram, thinks via DeepSeek, speaks via ElevenLabs |
| 3 | System Prompt | `system-prompt.txt` | 6-step structured interview protocol loaded into Joinly via `--system-prompt` |
| 4 | Transcript saved | — | Full Joinly log written to `/tmp/hermes-meeting-<timestamp>/joinly.log` |
| 5 | Summary generated | `generate_summary.py` | Parses the log, extracts speaker turns, estimates duration, produces structured Markdown |
| 6 | Memory saved | `supermemory.py` | Appends meeting data to `/root/.hermes/meeting-memory.json` |
| 7 | Telegram delivery | `hermes` CLI | Sends summary via `hermes send` to configured Telegram channel |

**File map:**
| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/hermes-meeting.sh` | Main entrypoint — orchestrates all 7 steps |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/.env.full` | Full env with all API keys (Deepgram, ElevenLabs, DeepSeek) |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/hermes-mcp-server.py` | MCP context server (JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout) |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/Dockerfile.mcp` | Dockerfile for the MCP server image |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/system-prompt.txt` | 6-step interview protocol |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/supermemory.py` | Long-term meeting memory storage |
| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/generate_summary.py` | Post-meeting summary generator |
| `/root/.hermes/meeting-memory.json` | Persistent memory store — accumulates across sessions |

**System prompt key content (6-step protocol):**
1. Introduction (30s) — greet client
2. Business Understanding (3-5min) — goals, audience, competitors
3. Project Scope (3-5min) — features, examples, current site
4. Timeline & Budget (2min) — ideal timeline, budget range
5. Technical Requirements (2min) — existing assets, integrations, maintenance
6. Closing (1min) — summarize, confirm next steps

**Memory commands:**
```bash
python3 supermemory.py                           # status: show client count, meeting count
python3 supermemory.py get "client-name"          # past meetings with that client
python3 supermemory.py recent                     # last 5 meetings
python3 supermemory.py save <transcript-logfile>  # save a meeting transcript
```

**Cost:** Same as Joinly alone (~$0.61/hr for API usage). All upgrades are self-hosted on the VPS — no additional subscriptions.

## Integration Points

### With Meeting Transcription (Vexa + Speaches)

The LiveKit voice agent and Vexa meeting recorder are independent:
- **Vexa** → records and transcribes meetings (post-meeting)
- **AgentCall** → joins meetings and talks in real-time
- **LiveKit** → one-on-one voice chat with AI

They can be combined by pointing the AI agent (via AgentCall) at the same meeting that Vexa is recording — the Vexa recording serves as a backup transcript while AgentCall handles the live conversation.

### With Hermes

The `ask_hermes` function tool in the voice agent allows the vocal AI to delegate tasks to Hermes' backend. This means you can say "Ask Hermes to research this project" and the voice agent forwards the request to Hermes' API endpoint.

### GitHub MCP Server (Repo Access for Agents)

**GitHub's official MCP Server** (29,660⭐) gives agents full read/write access to repos. Deployed as a Docker container that Hermes connects to via `docker exec -i`.

**Deployment:**
```bash
docker run -d --name github-mcp \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  --network agent-net \
  -e GITHUB_TOKEN="ghp_your_token" \
  node:20-slim \
  sh -c "
    mkdir -p /app && cd /app
    npm init -y >/dev/null 2>&1
    npm install @modelcontextprotocol/server-github >/dev/null 2>&1
    while true; do cat /dev/null; sleep 60; done
  "
```

**Container exits immediately with "running on stdio"** — The server-github MCP package expects to read from stdin/write to stdout. It's designed to be invoked per-request by an MCP host (like Hermes). The container just needs to keep alive for the package to be installed — the actual tool calls happen via `docker exec -i` which provides stdin/stdout per invocation.

**Tools available (42+):**
- `create_or_update_file`, `push_files` — write to repos
- `create_repository`, `fork_repository` — manage repos
- `search_repositories`, `search_code`, `get_me` — search and read
- `create_pull_request`, `merge_pull_request` — PR management
- `create_issue`, `list_issues`, `add_issue_comment` — issue tracking

**Wiring into Hermes config.yaml:**
```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    transport: stdio
    command: docker
    args:
      - exec
      - "-i"
      - github-mcp
      - sh
      - "-c"
      - "cd /app && node node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/server-github/dist/index.js"
```

**Container keeps alive with infinite sleep** — the process in the container does nothing (sleep loop). The `docker exec -i` call spawns a new node process per invocation.

**Pitfall: GITHUB_TOKEN vs GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN** — The server-github package reads `GITHUB_TOKEN`. If you use `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN`, the env var is ignored and auth fails silently. Set BOTH in the container environment:
```bash
-e GITHUB_TOKEN="ghp_xxx" \
-e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN="ghp_xxx"
```

**Testing the connection (from host):**
```bash
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}' | timeout 10 docker exec -i github-mcp sh -c "cd /app && node node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/server-github/dist/index.js" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.loads(sys.stdin.read()); print(f'Tools: {len(d[\"result\"][\"tools\"])}')"
```

### Per-Client Agent System

The `create-agent.py` tool at `/root/ai-agents/` creates dedicated AI agents per client repo. Each agent has:

- **GitHub repo access** (via GitHub MCP server)
- **Client profile** (business info, contact, goals)
- **Structured interview questions** (10 questions covering business, scope, timeline, budget)
- **Meeting memory** (past transcripts and summaries, persistent)
- **Custom system prompt** (agent's role, responsibilities, interview protocol)

**Creating a client agent:**
```bash
python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py create <client-name> <repo-url> '<client-info>'
```

**Example — Nexa Paraguay agent:**
```bash
python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py create "nexa-paraguay" \
  "https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay" \
  'Client: Sonia. Business: Helps Dutch expats relocate to Paraguay. Services: residency visas, property, banking. Website: nexa.paragu-ai.com (4 languages).'
```

**Agent files created:**
```
/root/ai-agents/nexa-paraguay/
  ├── profile.json        # Client info, interview questions, meeting history
  ├── system-prompt.txt   # Complete AI prompt for this client
  └── meetings.md         # Running log of all meetings
```

**Checking agents:**
```bash
python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py list
# nexa-paraguay     Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay     0 meetings
# elviajero-comercio Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio 0 meetings

python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py context nexa-paraguay
# Returns: CLIENT, REPO, INFO, PAST MEETINGS, QUESTIONS TO ASK TODAY
```

**Agent MCP Server** (`/root/ai-agents/agent-mcp-server.py`) exposes agents as MCP tools for Joinly/Hermes:
- `list_agents` — list all clients
- `get_agent` — full context for a client
- `save_meeting` — store a meeting transcript

### Full Hermes Meeting Bot Pipeline (May 2026 Build)

All upgrades in place. The complete meeting bot with context, memory, and delivery lives at `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/`. Entrypoints:


**What runs in sequence (full pipeline):**

| Step | Component | What it does |

| 7 | Telegram delivery | Sent via `hermes send` to configured channel |

**File map:**\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/hermes-meeting.sh` | Main entrypoint — orchestrates all 7 steps |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/.env.full` | Full env: Deepgram, ElevenLabs, DeepSeek keys |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/hermes-mcp-server.py` | MCP context server (JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout) |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/system-prompt.txt` | 6-step interview protocol (intro → biz → scope → timeline → tech → close) |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/supermemory.py` | Long-term meeting memory (`save`, `get`, `recent` commands) |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/generate_summary.py` | Post-meeting summary generator |\n| `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/repo_context.py` | Pre-fetches repo README, structure, and config files to inject into Joinly's system prompt |
| `/root/ai-agents/` | Per-client agent profiles (profile.json + system-prompt.txt + meetings.md) |
| `/root/ai-agents/create-agent.py` | Create/manage client agents |

**System prompt key content (6-step protocol):**

## Cluster Analysis

### Cluster 1: Meeting Infrastructure (3 skills)
- `meeting-bot-deployment` (operations) — user-facing operations view
- `self-hosted-meeting-infrastructure` (devops) — detailed deployment, Speaches, Vexa
- `voice-ai-infrastructure` (devops) — LiveKit, AgentCall, Joinly, TTS
- **Analysis:** `meeting-bot-deployment` already cross-references the other two. The three are naturally complementary views of the same domain. Merge into a single umbrella.

### Cluster 2: Hermes Ecosystem (2 skills)
- `hermes-ecosystem-audit` (dogfood) — research phase
- `hermes-ecosystem-installation` (devops) — installation & verification
- **Analysis:** Audit feeds into Installation. Merge into one.

### Cluster 3: Client Sales/Pricing/Documents (4 skills)
- `client-pricing-proposals` — pricing options for prospects
- `client-proposal-document` — proposal doc format
- `client-sales-pitch` — named-prospect pitch building
- **Analysis:** These all serve the same workflow: prospects → pricing → proposal → pitch. Merge into one umbrella.

### Cluster 4: Client Intelligence & Analysis (4 skills)
- `client-business-analysis` — full business model analysis
- `client-discovery-processor` — raw meeting outputs → structured docs
- `client-project-status` — comprehensive project analysis
- `client-docs-restructure` — docs cleanup/restructuring
- **Analysis:** Discovery → analysis → docs restructuring → status reporting is a pipeline. Merge.

### Cluster 5: Identity/Persona (2 skills)
- `agent-persona-design` — design agent identities
- `ai-whisperers-identity` — the canonical Ai-Whisperers identity system
- **Analysis:** `ai-whisperers-identity` is actually a specific INSTANCE of `agent-persona-design`. `agent-persona-design` is the class-level umbrella. Merge `ai-whisperers-identity` into it as a references entry.

### Cluster 6: Next.js Website Types (4 skills)
- `creative-portfolio-website` — artist portfolio
- `restaurant-website-nextjs` — restaurant menu site
- `event-website-sveltekit` — events (different framework, Next.js vs SvelteKit)
- `professional-services-website` — listed in related_skills but not in this skill set
- **Analysis:** These are genuinely different site archetypes. They share patterns (Tailwind, Docker, content JSON) but the workflows are distinct enough to keep separate. However, `tailwind-v4-arbitrary-values` is a reference that all three could use.

### Cluster 7: Next.js Technical Skills (3 skills)
- `nextjs-content-audit` — content-driven site audit
- `nextjs-docker-swarm` — deployment
- `nextjs-pages-to-app-router-migration` — migration
- **Analysis:** These share a Next.js focus but address different lifecycle stages. They're more complementary than overlapping. Let's keep them separate.

### Cluster 8: Market Research (2 skills)
- `org-intelligence-gathering` — person/entity across org
- `py-market-research` — Paraguay market vertical deep-dive
- `business-workflow-ai-audit` — business workflow audit
- **Analysis:** Different scopes. `org-intelligence-gathering` and `py-market-research` are both research skills but one is internal (find everything about a person) and one is external (find companies in a market). They're distinct.

### Cluster 9: Infrastructure/DevOps (4 skills)
- `api-proxy-deployment` — LiteLLM proxy
- `site-healthchecks` — healthcheck scripts
- `team-access-provisioning` — SSH keys

{"action": "patch", "name": "hermes-ecosystem-installation", "old_string": "## Pitfalls", "new_string": "## Ecosystem Audit Sub-Skill (Research Phase)

This section, absorbed from `hermes-ecosystem-audit`, feeds the research/intelligence phase before installation. Run this first to discover what's available, then use the main skill body to install.

### 1. Source Discovery

Always start with the canonical sources in priority order:

1. **Hermes Atlas** (hermesatlas.com) — community map of 110+ repos across 12 categories. Browse with `browser_navigate`, extract with `browser_snapshot`.
2. **Awesome Hermes Agent** (github.com/0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent, 2.7K★) — curated list. Use `web_extract` or `web_search`.
3. **OnlyTerp's Optimization Guide** (github.com/OnlyTerp/hermes-optimization-guide) — 23 parts, 13 installable skills, 5 config templates.
4. **Web search** for "hermes agent [category]" queries to catch tools not on Atlas.

### 2. Catalog & Classify

For each tool found, classify using:

| If... | Then... |
|-------|---------|
| Duplicates Hermes built-in | SKIP — note why |
| Offers NEW capability | CATALOG — rank by value |
| Already installed | MARK as ✅ |
| Reference/research only | BROWSE — extract takeaways |
| Windows-only guide | SKIP |

### 3. Priority Ranking

| Priority | Criteria | Examples |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **P0** | High impact, low effort, no deps | Config tweaks, skill installs |
| **P1** | Medium impact, medium effort, has deps | Memory provider, web UI | 
| **P2** | Nice to have, high effort, or niche | New MCP servers, cosmetic tools |

### 4. Pitfalls from Ecosystem Audit

- **Hermes Atlas renders dynamically** — use `browser_navigate` + `browser_snapshot`.
- **DO NOT delegate ecosystem research to weak models** (groq-llama, gemini-flash). Use deepseek-chat or better.
- **Star counts change fast** — always pull live data.
- **GitHub API rate limits** — unauthenticated requests hit 60/hr. Use `gh api` for bulk queries.
- **Always include current setup inventory** so the user can see what they already have vs what's new.
- **Prefer prune-before-install** — if skill library is large (200+), identify duplicates and unused skills first.

## Pitfalls"}

### Source Discovery (priority order)

1. **Hermes Atlas** (hermesatlas.com) — 110+ repos across 12 categories. `browser_navigate` + `browser_snapshot`.
2. **Awesome Hermes Agent** (github.com/0xNyk/awesome-hermes-agent, 2.7K★) — `web_extract` or `web_search`.
3. **OnlyTerp's Optimization Guide** (github.com/OnlyTerp/hermes-optimization-guide) — 23 parts, 13 skills, 5 config templates.
4. **Web search** — "hermes agent [category]" queries for tools not on Atlas.

### Classification Rules

| If... | Then... |
|-------|---------|
| Duplicates built-in | SKIP — note why |
| Offers NEW capability | CATALOG — rank by value |
| Already installed | MARK as ✅ in inventory |
| Reference/research only | BROWSE — extract takeaways |
| Windows-only | SKIP |

### Priority Ranking

| Priority | Criteria | Examples |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **P0** | High impact, low effort, no deps | Config tweaks, skill installs |
| **P1** | Medium impact, medium effort, has deps | Memory provider, web UI deploy |
| **P2** | Nice to have, high effort, or niche | New MCP servers, cosmetic tools |

### Deliverable

Produce a structured report with:
1. **Current state** — what we already have (MCPs, plugins, hooks, cron, config)
2. **Top ecosystem people** — key contributors and what they built
3. **P0 items** — specific tools + install commands + config diffs
4. **P1 items** — with effort estimate and prerequisite setup
5. **P2 items** — notes for future consideration

### Audit-Specific Pitfalls

- **Hermes Atlas renders dynamically** — `browser_navigate` + `browser_snapshot` works, `web_extract` may time out.
- **DO NOT delegate ecosystem research to weak models** (groq-llama, gemini-flash). Use deepseek-chat or better.
- **Star counts change fast** — always pull live data, don't rely on cached counts.
- **GitHub API rate limits** — unauthenticated: 60/hr. Use `gh api` (authenticated) for bulk queries.
- **Always include current setup inventory** so the user can see what they already have vs what's new.
- **Prefer prune-before-install** — if skill library is large (200+), identify duplicates and unused skills first.

## Common Pitfalls"}

This section documents the specific instance of persona design for Ai-Whisperers. It's a worked example of how the class-level skill above produces a real identity.

### Canonical Naming — Erebus

**The canonical name IS Erebus.** Chosen by Ivan on [REDACTED_PHONE]. See `references/ai-whisperers-soul.md` for the current SOUL.md.

**Meaning:** In Greek mythology, Erebus is the primordial darkness — the space between Chaos and creation where raw potential becomes light. Perfect metaphor for an AI workforce lead.

**Rejected names:** Verá (Guarani — rejected: "don't be guarani"), Sunstein (rejected: already a repo name).

**Technical identifiers (not personas):** `@ArchMagusBot` (Telegram), `agentzero` (VPS hostname), `sunstein.cloud` (infrastructure domain).

### Core Identity Files

| File | Purpose | Location |
|------|---------|----------|
| **SOUL.md** | Root persona definition | `~/.hermes/SOUL.md` |
| **BOOT.md** | Gateway startup behavior | `~/.hermes/BOOT.md` |
| **Config personalities** | Named persona profiles | `~/.hermes/config.yaml` → `personalities:` |
| **MCP Memory Server** | Knowledge graph | MCP tool `memory-server` |

### Org Context (Quick Facts)

- **Founder/Lead:** Ivan (ParaguAI)
- **Sales/Marketing Lead:** Kiki (Kyrian)
- **VPS:** Hostinger | 72.61.44.159 | 31GB RAM | 387GB disk | 8 vCPUs | Ubuntu 24.04.4
- **Deployment:** Docker Swarm + Traefik v3.5.3 + Cloudflare
- **Active sites:** 28+ client sites on paragu-ai.com subdomains
- **Hermes:** v0.13.0 | Gateway active | 16 MCPs | 956 skills | 10 cron jobs
- **Telegram:** @ArchMagusBot | **WhatsApp:** Evolution API at evolution.sunstein.cloud
- **Monitoring:** Grafana at monitor.paragu-ai.com | **Web chat:** hermes-chat.paragu-ai.com

### Team Agents (4 Profiles — May 2026)

| Agent | Profile Name | Primary User | Model |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| Explorer-Bot | `explorer-bot` | Ivan/Erebus | DeepSeek |
| Closer-Bot | `closer-bot` | Kiki (Kyrian) | DeepSeek + Claude |
| Architect-Bot | `architect-bot` | Erebus/Ivan | DeepSeek |
| Copy-Bot | `copy-bot` | Kiki (Kyrian) | DeepSeek |

Each profile at `~/.hermes/profiles/<name>/` with its own config.yaml, SOUL.md, and .env.

### SOUL.md Authoring Pattern for Ai-Whisperers

**What to include:**
1. Agent name — **Erebus** (confirmed). Do NOT change without Ivan's explicit request.
2. Origin — "AI workforce lead for Ai-Whisperers."
3. **Voice** — Calm, direct, senior engineer. Coworker not bot. Ship first, explain if needed. No "as an AI" apologies. Spanish+English naturally mixed.
4. **Platform adaptations** — WhatsApp: 3 sentences max, bullet points, no markdown, zero greetings. TUI/CLI: no preamble.
5. **Prohibited** — No "as an AI", no "I apologize, but", no excessive hedging, no kaomoji/emoji unless user uses them first.
6. **Work ethos** — "I own the problem." Fix everything then report. Root cause chains, not status reports. Architecture-first. Batch operations. Product thinking.
7. **Relationships** — Ivan is founder. Human team are coworkers.
8. **Sub-personas** — Erebus-Dev, Erebus-Ops, Erebus-Research, Erebus-Client (future).

**What NOT to include in SOUL.md:**
- Detailed org inventory (repos, Docker services) — that belongs in MCP memory
- Task-specific instructions (those go in skills)
- Platform-specific tool config (goes in `config.yaml` → `platform_toolsets:`)
- Environment variables or secrets

**SOUL.md Rewrite Workflow:**
1. The canonical name is **Erebus**. If user wants to change it, confirm intent.
2. Draft the SOUL.md, write with `cat > ~/.hermes/SOUL.md`
3. Update platform display names (Telegram, WhatsApp Evolution profile, Open WebUI)
4. Update memory to reflect the name change
5. Re-seed the knowledge graph with updated agent entity

{"content": "   360|- **Gateway per profile is not automatic.** By default, new profiles have `gateway: stopped`. To enable gateway for a profile, configure its `config.yaml` accordingly. Without it, profiles are CLI-only.
   361|- **SOUL.md too generic:** If it's just "be concise" + "use cheap model", there's no identity. Every persona needs at minimum Identity + Voice + Work Ethos.
   362|- **Knowledge graph not seeded:** Without it, the agent rediscovers the org every session — wastes time and context. See `references/knowledge-graph-seeding.md` for the full entity/relation template.
   363|- **Sub-personas assumed too early:** Start with one well-defined main persona. Let sub-personas emerge from actual usage patterns, not theory.
   364|- **Platform rules not specific:** "Be concise" means different things on WhatsApp (3 sentences max) vs TUI (no preamble).
   365|- **Culture-bound naming:** If the team is multinational, avoid names that are meaningful in only one language/culture.
   366|- **Over-mythologizing:** A name from myth should reference a *concept* that fits the role, not be a literal god. The agent is a coworker, not a deity.
   367|- **Don't iterate on a rejected theme:** User says "don't use Paraguay/Guarani names" → do NOT propose another Guarani name. Move to a completely different direction. You only get one chance per frame.
   368|- **Re-propose previously engaged names:** If a name was discussed in a past session and the user engaged with it (even briefly), it's fair game to re-propose in a new session. The user's memory of it is net-positive — they already accepted the concept even if the timing wasn't right then.
   369|- **Rejected positions can be recovered:** The user rejected Erebus during a prior naming discussion (happened in the same session where Paraguay-themed names were rejected), then re-proposed it themselves later. If the user brings back a name you suggested earlier, run with it — they've decided it works.

   371|## Verification

   373|After creating a persona:

   376|3. Send a test message via WhatsApp/TUI and verify the response matches the defined voice
   377|4. Confirm the name is NOT a repo in the org: search GitHub org for the name
